If you have completed the exercises from part 1, you should have refined list of your expertise, skills, and experience and have an initial idea for a minimum viable audience that could benefit from what you already know or can acquire through learning and practice. Don't worry if what you've done so far is not perfect. We're going to iterate our way to success, and all you need now is a starting point to take action. We want to immerse ourselves completely into the needs and desires of our minimum viable audience and capture how those needs and desires are expressed by that audience, in their own words, thinking, and observed behavior. We'll compile those insights over time in documents, empathy maps, and value proposition canvases. It's important to be clear that we can't tell you exactly how to use the list of resources below because we don't know the way in which your interests, experience, and abilities intersect with your intended audience. You may be leaning toward a physical product, for example, which would be different than researching services, which is different than researching digital products, etc. We're going to give you guidelines and resources that you will need to experiment with and make your own. Again, perfection is the enemy, progress is the goal. The common thread among all of the resources we'll share as we're researching our audiences in media where those audiences communicate their needs and desires as individuals. For example, Amazon reviews, Facebook groups, Reddit threads, Twitter conversations, and medium article comments give us opportunities to understand the needs and desires of audiences we may want to serve. Each of those examples, and more in our list below, are helpful because we have access to the opinions of individuals expressed in their own words. Tools like POSUMO are also useful because they aggregate data, but that's not the same as capturing ideas directly from individuals in their own words. One final note before we dive in, you do not need to explore every resource in this list. That's a really effective way to procrastinate, and that's not our intent. Instead, we want you to pick the most appropriate resources for your specific audience and then get started. You'll likely find significant, useful insights from two to three sources, which you'll be able to confirm or disconfirm using the validate process later. Being your audience versus researching an audience. From experience, we can say there is a world of difference between being one of your audience and someone just researching an audience they are not a member of. Writing a short piece of fiction will reveal more about what you don't know than reading five books on the craft of writing or just being a fly on the wall of a writing form or group. We can't overstate how different these two dynamics are. Thinking about being a programmer is different from writing a program that does something useful, which will reveal everything you don't know. Researching about how to build a racing simulator is nothing like actually building a racing simulator, which will reveal nuances you would have never considered. The point is, being your own customer, solving your own problems, pains, gains, and jobs to be done, is one of the few little secrets worth paying attention to. It sticks out like a stake stand at a vegan convention when we see marketers trying to sell something they have zero experience in, no results of their own or problem they've solved themselves. Don't be that person. Become your own customer of one, then pull on the threads that emerge and reveal themselves. Amazon Reviews. Amazon product reviews can be a goldmine of insights about buying motivations, expectations, and features, benefits that really matter. To start, find products that help you locate the audience you hope to serve. For example, find the top three to five books about a topic that interests your audience, read the reviews, and look for patterns. What do people complain about? What do they mention as being important? Why did they buy the book in the first place? What problem were they trying to solve? Where did the author succeed? Where did the author fall short? Often you'll find links to other books about that topic too, with reasons why one book is better, or, occasionally, worse, according to the reviewer. That's a very effective way to find the most impactful books for a particular subject, which begins to create a map of meaningful resources for you to investigate. To be clear, we're not suggesting you read book reviews so you can write a better book. Instead, we know that books are a common method people use to find solutions to problems and explore meaningful interests. We're using books, in this example, as a proxy, entry point into the deeper topics our audience is interested in. Let's consider an example. In 2016, Tim Ferriss published Tools of Titans, The Tactics, Routines, and Habits of Billionaires, Icons, and World-Class Performers. The reviews for Tools of Titans are a treasure trove of insights about what Tim's personal development-oriented audience was expecting, and, many, specific examples explaining where and how the author didn't deliver on those expectations. Here's one review, on the first page, that's particularly interesting. It's also important to notice that 3,600-plus people found that review helpful. That's a lot of social proof confirming that the themes that reviewer mentioned resonate with, many, others. Tools of Titans is an extreme example. Tim Ferriss has an enormous personal development-oriented audience and his books are popular. You're not going to find that in every market and that's okay. You're looking for themes and insights, reviews or wonderful resources to find both. If you're considering creating a physical product, use the same process but focus on products that fulfill the same needs, meet the same desires you're interested in. Mind the reviews to find problems with current solutions, suggestions to improve existing products, and other valuable information. When you read reviews, you're looking for recurring themes, and you want to capture those themes in the language of your audience. Two and four star reviews are fertile grounds to mind for themes and insights where the author, or product, fell short in some meaningful way. Facebook and LinkedIn groups. Facebook groups and LinkedIn groups are broadly useful for audience research. Early on in the process, the first few times through the TDB flywheel, we're just trying to gather data and organize it into coherent themes to identify a minimum viable audience and their specific jobs, pains, and gains. Don't obsess about finding and joining every relevant group, you'll drive yourself crazy with overwhelm quickly. Instead, look for three to five groups that seem like they're good matches and then get to work reading posts and comments. Initially, you want to confirm your group selection is a good match. Read 20 to 30 posts and the comments, and decide if you found your audience. If not, move on to another group and repeat the process. You're looking for the questions people ask, the issues they're struggling with, the answers they get in the comments, resources they're referred to by others, and anything else that is useful to help you better understand their needs and desires. You'll also want to determine the sophistication level of the audience. Are they newbies just getting started or seasoned veterans who are very familiar with the topic? Context matters. This is important because one of the decisions you'll need to make is where your ideal audience is on the spectrum of least to most sophisticated. Keep in mind that you need to be an expert to sell to experts, and it's much easier to sell mid-level expertise to novices, and there are many more novices in markets too. However, your situation will be unique, just keep audience sophistication in mind when you're evaluating posts and comments. Reddit threads and forms. Reddit threads and online forms can be useful audience research tools. Start by searching Reddit for threads using broad keyword phrases, then narrow your search as you begin to uncover the Reddit landscape for that topic. To find forms, search topic plus form on Google or your favorite search engine. There's no right way to do this so experiment and find what works for you and the audience you're researching. It's worth the effort to find Reddit discussions and forms. They're often incredible sources of deep insights about audience needs and desires. We found that searching for AMAs, ask me anything, is a particularly good way to mine for themes and insights. Below is an AMA by Aaron Sorgan, Reddit link here, the writer of the West Wing. There are over 3,000 comments of budding authors and screenwriters asking a master of his craft questions. Stop and consider how valuable that is, especially when the expert was interacting with his fans. Reddit has an entire subreddits for AMAs, another rabbit hole of audience insights to mine for gold. Once you've found a few threads, forms, immerse yourself in those for an hour or two pulling on threads and see where they lead. Again, we're looking for audience insights, notice, and capture in your notes when you discover recurring themes. Twitter? Twitter is an amazing research tool, and, like other social media, it's also a potential time waster so be careful. We recommend that you start broadly using Twitter's search function and then use Twitter lists to curate your collection of Twitter accounts to follow. Buffer has a good article on getting the most from Twitter lists, 23 seldom used ideas for how to use Twitter lists. Zapier's ultimate guide to Twitter advanced search is a good place to start learning how to use Twitter for research. Other? There are many other sources of audience information online. However, we want to find the right balance between quality and quantity of information and it's very, very easy to become overwhelmed with quantity. Consider these as options that may apply to specific audiences. If they're relevant for your audience, that's great. If not, ignore them and move on. Medium reader comments. Search articles by topic and skim comments for useful information. Instagram, content and comments. Kickstarter, comments and FAQs. Kickstarter, and similar crowdfunding platforms, can be particularly useful for physical products. In-person summits and masterminds. COVID aside, it can't be overstated how valuable in-person niche gatherings are at uncovering a world of intimate audience insights. Nuances that only reveal themselves within the hubbub of these closed events. I, Andre, was invited to speak at the Smarter Artist Summit in 2016. I don't do speaking engagements, but I begrudgingly accepted as a favor to my three friends whose event this was. I'm so glad I attended. Below is my three D point of view of the crowd who lobbed questions like hand grenades during the AMA session. It was a room full of authors of varying caliber, from wannabes to New York Times number one best sellers. The most surprising theme that showed up was that the broad majority of authors, regardless of their success and expertise, are petrified to death of using email as a channel to sell their books. This theme was validated repeatedly during the two days as I spoke to people over coffee at lunch and beers at dinner. I think some insights like this can be challenging to parse out on online public forums and groups. Yet, at events like this, people feel safe, so they share openly and vulnerably. Smarter Artist Summit, Austin, April 2016. Smarter Artist Summit, Austin, April 2016. The single word to describe this level of interaction is immersion. Be curious, ask questions. The other insight I discovered was, no matter how experienced an author was, they had pains they were struggling with, and gains they were seeking to move towards. No author was immune to having significant flaws and issues that kept them up at night. This is a universal truth across any audience segment. Broadly speaking, online forums and groups aren't populated by experts in their craft. You won't find Stephen King in a Facebook group lolling with fans. But events like this are attended by experts within their category of expertise, and they're almost always accessible. After the two-day main event, I attended a high-level mastermind. There were perhaps 17, 6 and 7 figure authors in the room. It was so interesting to see how similar the experts were to the ordinary authors. They used the same services, email, book funnels, publishing platforms, tools, methods of promotion, etc. The only difference was in the nuances and the context. But even then, many of their pains and gains were thematically similar to what everyone else was struggling with. Compilation. Use these platforms to find where your minimum viable audience is spending time and discover what matters to them, the problems they care enough about to try and solve, etc. The above list certainly isn't exhaustive, but it'll give you a great head start and traction. See where the journey of discovery and deep dive fly on the wall observation takes you. Allow it to take you wherever it takes you. On a meta-level you're looking for social communities, wide and narrow, where people are hanging out and connecting with each other. Overwhelm is a very serious issue, especially when you're first getting started. We recommend that you use time blocking to minimize overwhelm and maintain momentum and focus. For example, spend one hour per day researching your audience five days a week, or whatever is right for you. Use the time constraint as a forcing mechanism, and approach the process like a treasure hunt. You're looking for the highest value places your audience congregates, and then you're mining those places for the highest value insights you can find. Capture those insights simply at first, Google Docs, Word, or a more sophisticated tool like Evernote will work. We'll recommend more advanced tools later, for now, we don't want any barriers to progress. Don't worry about trying to capture these insights exactly the right way, just capture them. Start making lists, and you'll begin to sense what's important because you'll see it more often. Patterns will emerge when you immerse yourself in the process. In part three we'll discuss how to engage with individuals directly for more nuanced feedback, and to fill in any details that your research has not yet revealed. Action items. One, identify two to three resources from the options above to mind for audience insights. Two, establish time constraints one week at a time. For example, in week one commit to 45 minutes of audience research per day and stay committed to that constraint. Don't spend more or less time. Three, create a simple document to begin capturing insights. Google Docs, Word, Evernote, etc. Do not obsess about using the right tool or the optimal method. All you need at this stage is a digital container to capture insights that seem interesting. Four, focus on one resource per day and immerse yourself in it. Keep pulling on threads and be willing to abandon any resources that aren't valuable. Five, whenever you find something interesting, copy it into your document. Categorize very lightly organically when you see the same themes appearing group those comments together in your document. Don't try to force a structure, let that emerge over time. Use the private accountability community to have discussions with your peers and share your insights and your work. For questions, please use the comments below. Once again, please don't show us your work for critique, we can't possibly offer individual feedback for everyone. Most importantly, this is work you need to do to extract insights that will be meaningful only to you deep in the trenches, a level of nuance that will be invisible to us offering any level of critique. Andre and Sean
